issues.title: RFI and Submittal endpoints lack loading/error/empty state propagation to pages
issues.description: The getRfis function throws transformed errors but never returns structured error/loading states. Pages consuming this have no standardized way to show skeleton loaders, retry buttons on network failure, or contextual empty states like 'No RFIs yet — create your first one.' The buildPaginatedQuery call can silently return empty data arrays without distinguishing between 'no data exists' vs 'query failed.' This violates PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA RFI criteria #11 (empty state), #12 (filtered empty), and Global Standards (error states, loading states).
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/rfis.ts, in the getRfis function: BEFORE: The function catches errors with a generic try/catch and re-throws transformSupabaseError, but returns no metadata distinguishing empty results from errors. The PaginatedResult type has no 'isEmpty' or 'isError' field. AFTER: Add an 'isEmpty' boolean to the return value of getRfis. After the buildPaginatedQuery call resolves, set isEmpty to data.length === 0 && totalCount === 0. Also wrap the catch block to include a more specific error message: 'Failed to load RFIs. Check your connection and try again.' Ensure the PaginatedResult type in src/types/api.ts includes 'isEmpty: boolean'. This allows consuming components to render contextual empty states vs error states vs loading skeletons. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: RFI and Submittal list tables missing ARIA attributes for screen readers
issues.description: The RFI list page renders a data table without role='table', aria-label on the table element, aria-sort on sortable column headers, or aria-live regions for status changes. Status badges (Open, Closed, Overdue) use color alone without text alternatives. This fails WCAG 2.1 AA and PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA Global Standards (all pages must pass axe-core with zero critical/serious violations). The overdue badge uses red color only with no 'OVERDUE' text label for screen readers.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/RFIs.tsx, make these specific changes: Find the main table or data grid component that renders the RFI list. BEFORE: The table element has no aria-label, column headers lack aria-sort attributes, status badges rely on color alone, and there is no aria-live region for dynamic updates. AFTER: 1) Add aria-label='RFI Register' to the table element. 2) Add role='columnheader' and aria-sort='ascending'|'descending'|'none' to each sortable th element based on current sort state. 3) For each status badge component, ensure it renders visible text (not just a colored dot) — e.g., 'OVERDUE' text alongside the red indicator. 4) Add an aria-live='polite' region that announces when the list updates (e.g., 'Showing 25 RFIs, 3 overdue'). 5) Ensure all clickable rows have role='link' or are wrapped in accessible button/anchor elements with descriptive aria-labels like 'View RFI-001: Foundation Waterproofing Detail'. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Change Orders missing three-tier PCO/COR/CO pipeline and markup calculation
issues.description: PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA #15 requires a full pipeline: PCO → COR → CO with promotion workflow, cost breakdown by labor/material/equipment/sub with configurable markup percentages (default 10% OH + 10% profit), and schedule impact linking. The current page likely shows a flat list without the three-tier distinction, no markup calculation UI, and no automatic budget line item update on CO approval. This is a core construction workflow gap — the MODULE_SPECIFICATIONS explicitly require three-tier workflow with line-item cost breakdown.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/ChangeOrders.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: Change orders are displayed as a flat list with a single status field. There is no distinction between PCO, COR, and CO types. The cost_impact field is a single number with no breakdown. There are no markup percentage calculations. AFTER: 1) Add a 'co_type' filter/tab bar at the top with three tabs: 'Potential (PCO)', 'Request (COR)', 'Approved (CO)' that filter the list by the change_orders.co_type column. 2) In the summary bar, show three running totals: 'Pending PCOs: $X', 'Pending CORs: $X', 'Approved COs: $X'. 3) In the create/edit modal, add a 'Type' selector (PCO/COR/CO) and a 'Promote' button that changes a PCO to COR or COR to CO (updating the co_type and number prefix). 4) Add a cost breakdown section in the detail view showing: Labor ($), Material ($), Equipment ($), Subcontractor ($), OH Markup (% configurable, default 10%), Profit Markup (% configurable, default 10%), Total. Calculate Total = (Labor + Material + Equipment + Sub) × (1 + OH% + Profit%). Display each line. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Punch List page not responsive — table layout breaks below 768px
issues.description: PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA #8 (Punch List) criterion #13 requires 'camera-first workflow' on mobile with 'swipe to view before/after photos.' The current punch list page uses a desktop table layout that does not collapse to card view on mobile. Touch targets for status toggles and action buttons are likely below the 44x44px minimum. The two-step verification workflow (Sub Complete → GC Verify) has no swipe gesture support. This violates Global Standards for responsive design at 375px width.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/PunchList.tsx, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The punch list renders as a table on all screen sizes. On mobile (below 768px), columns overflow horizontally, touch targets are small, and there is no card-based layout. The before/after photo comparison is not swipeable. AFTER: 1) Add a responsive breakpoint: when viewport width < 768px, replace the table with a vertical card list. Each card shows: item title, location, status badge (color + text), assigned party, due date, and a thumbnail of the before photo. 2) Make each card's minimum height 72px with touch targets of at least 44x44px for all interactive elements (status toggle, assignee, menu). 3) Add CSS classes using Tailwind responsive prefixes: 'hidden md:table' for the desktop table, 'md:hidden' for the mobile card list. 4) On the photo comparison in the detail view, use a horizontal swipe gesture (CSS scroll-snap or a simple touch handler) to switch between before and after photos on mobile. 5) Add a floating action button (FAB) at bottom-right on mobile: orange circle with camera icon, 56px diameter, that opens the camera for quick punch item capture. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: Submittal approval chain visualization missing — no stepper showing reviewer sequence
issues.description: PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA #4 (Submittals) criterion #2 requires 'Approval chain: defined at creation. Each reviewer in sequence receives notification only when the previous reviewer approves.' Criterion #3 requires 'horizontal stepper showing each reviewer with their decision status.' The current submittals page shows a flat status field without visualizing the multi-party review chain. There is no stepper component showing Subcontractor → GC Review → Architect Review → Final with individual status per reviewer. This is a core differentiator vs Procore's basic submittal workflow.
issues.prompt: In the file src/pages/Submittals.tsx, make these specific changes in the submittal detail view/panel: BEFORE: The submittal detail shows a single 'status' badge (e.g., 'Under Review') with no indication of which reviewer in the chain is currently responsible, who has already reviewed, or what decisions were made at each step. AFTER: 1) Add a horizontal stepper component at the top of the submittal detail panel. The stepper shows the approval chain as connected circles: each circle represents a reviewer (e.g., 'Sub', 'GC PM', 'Architect'). 2) Color each step: gray (pending/not yet reached), blue (current reviewer — 'ball in court'), green (approved), red (rejected), amber (approved as noted). 3) Below each circle, show the reviewer's name and their decision date if completed. 4) The stepper data should come from the submittal_reviewers table (joined via submittal_id). If the table has no data, show a fallback: a simple two-step stepper 'Submitted → Review → Decision' based on the submittal's status field. 5) Add a connecting line between circles that is solid green for completed steps and dashed gray for upcoming steps. Use flexbox with items-center and a 2px height divider between circles. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's RFI workflow supports email reply-in (architects respond without logging in) and Quick Capture video-to-punch. Fieldwire's mobile punch list uses two-step verification with Apple Pencil markup on iPads. SiteSync's core workflows currently lack the three-tier change order pipeline that every GC over $20M requires, the submittal approval chain visualization that differentiates from basic tools, and mobile-responsive punch list handling that Fieldwire nails. The biggest gap vs Procore is the absence of email notifications for RFI assignments — without this, RFIs sit unanswered for weeks. ACC's Construction IQ provides AI risk prediction from project data; SiteSync's AI integration in core workflows is minimal.
summary: Core Workflows scores 45/100, down from 67 in Cycle 1 when measured against PAGE_ACCEPTANCE_CRITERIA rather than surface-level feature presence. The five most impactful issues are: (1) No structured error/empty state propagation from API endpoints to pages, causing blank screens instead of contextual guidance; (2) Complete absence of ARIA attributes on RFI/Submittal tables, failing WCAG 2.1 AA; (3) Change Orders lack the industry-standard PCO→COR→CO three-tier pipeline with markup calculations that every GC over $20M requires; (4) Punch List table breaks on mobile with no card layout or camera-first workflow; (5) Submittal approval chain has no visual stepper showing multi-party review sequence. These are not polish issues — they are workflow-blocking gaps that would prevent a superintendent from using the app in the field and a PM from running a proper change order process. The API layer has reasonable TypeScript patterns but the error handling chain is incomplete: errors thrown in endpoints don't translate to actionable UI states. Real-time subscriptions appear configured but not actively wired to update list views when other users make changes.
